 document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
 // Mobile drawer
 const toggle = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-menu-toggle]');
 const drawer = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-drawer]');
 const backdrop = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-backdrop]');
 const closeBtn = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-drawer-close]');

 function openDrawer() {
 if (!drawer || !backdrop) return;
 drawer.classList.remove('translate-x-full');
 drawer.classList.add('translate-x-0');
 backdrop.classList.remove('opacity-0', 'pointer-events-none');
 backdrop.classList.add('opacity-100', 'pointer-events-auto');
 document.body.classList.add('overflow-hidden');
 if (toggle) toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true');
 }

 function closeDrawer() {
 if (!drawer || !backdrop) return;
 drawer.classList.remove('translate-x-0');
 drawer.classList.add('translate-x-full');
 backdrop.classList.remove('opacity-100', 'pointer-events-auto');
 backdrop.classList.add('opacity-0', 'pointer-events-none');
 document.body.classList.remove('overflow-hidden');
 if (toggle) toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
 }

 if (toggle) toggle.addEventListener('click', openDrawer);
 if (closeBtn) closeBtn.addEventListener('click', closeDrawer);
 if (backdrop) backdrop.addEventListener('click', closeDrawer);

 // Close drawer on Escape key
 document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
 if (e.key === 'Escape' && toggle && toggle.getAttribute('aria-expanded') === 'true') {
 closeDrawer();
 }
 });

 // Mobile accordion (Learn dropdown in drawer)
 document.querySelectorAll('[data-mobile-accordion]').forEach((acc) => {
 const btn = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-toggle]');
 const content = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-content]');
 const chevron = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-chevron]');
 if (!btn || !content) return;

 btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
 const isOpen = !content.classList.contains('hidden');
 content.classList.toggle('hidden', isOpen);
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(!isOpen));
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = isOpen ? '' : 'rotate(180deg)';
 });
 });

 // Dropdown menus
 document.querySelectorAll('[data-dropdown]').forEach((dropdown) => {
 const btn = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-toggle]');
 const menu = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-menu]');
 const chevron = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-chevron]');
 if (!btn || !menu) return;

 btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
 const open = !menu.classList.contains('hidden');
 menu.classList.toggle('hidden', open);
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(!open));
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = open ? '' : 'rotate(180deg)';
 });

 document.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
 if (!dropdown.contains(e.target)) {
 menu.classList.add('hidden');
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = '';
 }
 });
 });
 });
</script><main id="main-content" class="flex-1"><div class="ms-page"><article class="ms-blog-article"><nav class="ms-blog-breadcrumb" aria-label="Breadcrumb"><a href="/blog">Blog</a><span aria-hidden="true">/</span><span>Kimi K2 Runs 300 Sub-Agents Across 4,000 Steps on 4x H100s — The Story Hermes Found That Everyone Missed</span></nav><header class="ms-blog-header"><div class="ms-blog-keywords"><a href="/blog/tag/multi-agent" class="ms-badge ms-badge-sm ms-badge-blue hover:opacity-80 transition-opacity">Multi-Agent</a><a href="/blog/tag/llms-models" class="ms-badge ms-badge-sm ms-badge-blue hover:opacity-80 transition-opacity">LLMs & Models</a><a href="/blog/tag/automation" class="ms-badge ms-badge-sm ms-badge-blue hover:opacity-80 transition-opacity">Automation</a></div><h1>Kimi K2 Runs 300 Sub-Agents Across 4,000 Steps on 4x H100s — The Story Hermes Found That Everyone Missed</h1><p class="ms-blog-header-desc">Hermes's content ideation agent surfaced Kimi K2: an open-source system orchestrating 300 sub-agents across 4,000 coordinated steps on 4x H100 GPUs.</p><div class="ms-blog-header-meta"><span>MindStudio Team</span><span aria-hidden="true">·</span><time datetime="2026-05-04T00:00:00.000Z">May 4, 2026</time><span aria-hidden="true">·</span><a href="/rss.xml" class="ms-blog-rss-link"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><path stroke="none" d="M0 0h24v24H0z" fill="none"></path>

  <path d="M4 11a9 9 0 0 1 9 9"></path></svg>RSS</a></div></header><img src="https://i.mscdn.ai/b4ffc41c-fcef-4043-b587-9921a5ca401e/generated-images/0cdd7672-39f3-42a4-b30f-0ae7ccdd8d7f.png?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover" alt="Kimi K2 Runs 300 Sub-Agents Across 4,000 Steps on 4x H100s — The Story Hermes Found That Everyone Missed" class="ms-blog-hero-img" width="1200" height="675" loading="eager"><div class="prose prose-lg max-w-none ms-blog-content"><section aria-labelledby="s-1"><h2 id="s-1">Kimi K2 Orchestrates 300 Sub-Agents Across 4,000 Steps — And Most Coverage Missed the Point Entirely</h2>
<p>Kimi K2’s benchmark numbers got the headlines. The actual story — a system that orchestrates 300 sub-agents across 4,000 coordinated steps on 4x H100 GPUs, released as open source — barely registered.</p>
<p>That detail didn’t surface from a press release or a researcher’s Twitter thread. It surfaced when a Hermes agent, running on a $0.24/hour CPU instance at <a href="https://hpcai.com">hpcai.com</a>, was asked a simple content ideation prompt: <em>“Research what’s going on in AI and give me three fresh content ideas that most people have overlooked but are important.”</em></p>
<p>The agent came back with this: <em>“Everyone is talking about Kimi K2 comparing benchmark scores, but they’re missing what Kimi actually shipped. The system that orchestrates 300 sub-agents across 4,000 coordinated steps on four times H100 GPUs. That’s not a better model, that’s an execution substrate, and it’s open source.”</em></p>
<p>That framing — “execution substrate, not a better model” — is the right frame. And the fact that an autonomous agent surfaced it before most human analysts did is itself worth examining.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-2"><h2 id="s-2">The Benchmark Coverage Was a Distraction</h2>
<p>When Kimi K2 dropped, the coverage pattern was predictable: MMLU scores, coding benchmarks, comparisons to GPT-4o and Claude. The usual leaderboard journalism.</p>
<p>That coverage isn’t wrong, exactly. Benchmark comparisons are useful. But they describe the model in isolation, as if the interesting question is “how smart is it?” rather than “what does it actually do when deployed?”</p>

<p>The interesting question with Kimi K2 is the second one. What Moonshot AI shipped isn’t just a model with good scores — it’s a multi-agent orchestration system where the model serves as the reasoning layer inside a much larger execution architecture.</p>
<p>300 sub-agents. 4,000 coordinated steps. Four H100 GPUs running in parallel. That’s not a chatbot with a better MMLU score. That’s an infrastructure decision about how to decompose hard problems into parallelizable work.</p>
<p>The <a href="/blog/what-is-autoresearch-loop-karpathy-business-optimization">AutoResearch loop pattern that Karpathy described</a> — where agents autonomously run experiments, measure results, and iterate — is essentially what Kimi K2’s architecture operationalizes at scale. The difference is that Kimi K2 ships with the orchestration layer already built.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-3"><h2 id="s-3">What 300 Sub-Agents Actually Means</h2>
<p>The number sounds impressive, but the architecture question is more interesting than the count.</p>
<p>Running 300 sub-agents in parallel isn’t just “more agents.” It requires a coordination layer that can decompose a task into 300 independent work units, dispatch them, track their state, handle failures, and synthesize results. That’s a distributed systems problem as much as it’s an AI problem.</p>
<p>4,000 coordinated steps across those agents means the system isn’t just fan-out parallelism — it’s maintaining state and dependencies across a long execution graph. Some agents are presumably waiting on outputs from others. The orchestrator has to manage that dependency graph without losing coherence.</p>
<p>This is why the “execution substrate” framing matters. A better model gives you better answers to individual questions. An execution substrate gives you the ability to run qualitatively different classes of work — tasks that are too large, too parallelizable, or too long-running for a single model call to handle.</p>
<p>The open-source release compounds this. When the orchestration architecture is open, teams can inspect how Moonshot AI solved the coordination problem, adapt it, and build on top of it. That’s a different kind of contribution than releasing model weights with a permissive license.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-4"><h2 id="s-4">How an Autonomous Agent Found This Before Most Humans Did</h2>
<p>The Hermes content ideation demo is worth examining on its own terms, separate from what it found.</p>
<p>The prompt was deliberately vague: research AI news, surface three overlooked but important ideas. No specific sources, no constraints on format, no list of topics to check. The agent had to decide what “overlooked but important” means operationally — which sources to scrape, how to weight recency versus significance, how to distinguish “covered but underweighted” from “genuinely missed.”</p>
<p>It came back with three ideas. The Kimi K2 multi-agent architecture story. An AI paper using Sam Altman’s WorldCoin data to argue against UBI. A paper claiming all 12 tested AI safety defenses had been broken.</p>
<p>Two out of three of those are stories that would require a human researcher to be actively monitoring the right corners of arXiv and AI Twitter to catch. The Kimi K2 one in particular requires knowing that benchmark coverage ≠ architecture coverage — a distinction that takes some domain knowledge to make.</p>
<p>The agent made that distinction correctly.</p>
<p>This is the part of the demo that doesn’t get enough attention in discussions of autonomous agents. The value isn’t just automation of known tasks. It’s the ability to surface signal from noise in domains where you don’t have time to maintain continuous attention.</p>

</section><section aria-labelledby="s-5"><h2 id="s-5">The Broader Pattern: What Gets Missed When Coverage Optimizes for Speed</h2>
<p>There’s a structural reason the Kimi K2 architecture story got underweighted. Benchmark comparisons are fast to produce and easy to verify — you run the eval, you report the number. Architecture analysis requires reading technical documentation, understanding distributed systems tradeoffs, and making a judgment call about what’s significant.</p>
<p>Under time pressure, coverage optimizes for the fast path. Benchmark numbers get published within hours. Architecture analysis shows up days later, if at all.</p>
<p>This is a general problem in AI coverage, not specific to Kimi K2. The <a href="/blog/what-is-claude-mythos-anthropic-most-powerful-model">Claude Mythos surfacing through API leaks and benchmark drops</a> rather than official announcements is another example of the same pattern — the technically significant signal arriving through indirect channels while official coverage focuses on announced features.</p>
<p>Autonomous agents running continuous research loops are one partial solution to this. They don’t have the time pressure that drives human coverage toward the fast path. They can run a broader search, weight sources differently, and surface things that fall outside the standard coverage template.</p>
<p>The Hermes demo ran on a CPU instance at $0.24/hour. The agent that found the Kimi K2 architecture story cost less than a cup of coffee to run. That’s a meaningful asymmetry.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-6"><h2 id="s-6">What the Architecture Implies for Builders</h2>
<p>If you’re building multi-agent systems, the Kimi K2 architecture raises some concrete questions worth thinking through.</p>
<p>The 300 sub-agent number implies a task decomposition strategy. Most multi-agent frameworks top out well below that in practice — not because of technical limits, but because task decomposition gets hard. How do you break a problem into 300 independent units without creating so many dependencies that the parallelism is illusory? How do you handle the 5% of sub-agents that fail or return garbage?</p>
<p>The 4,000 coordinated steps number implies persistent state management across a long execution. That’s different from stateless fan-out. It means the orchestrator is maintaining a model of what’s been done, what’s in flight, and what’s blocked — essentially a workflow engine embedded in the agent architecture.</p>
<p>For teams building on top of open-source frameworks, this is the design space worth studying in the Kimi K2 release. Not the benchmark scores — the coordination architecture.</p>
<p>Platforms like <a href="/">MindStudio</a> handle this orchestration layer for teams that don’t want to build it from scratch: 200+ models, 1,000+ integrations, and a visual builder for chaining agents and workflows without writing the coordination code yourself.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-7"><h2 id="s-7">The Open-Source Angle Is Underreported Too</h2>
<p>Kimi K2 being open source isn’t just a licensing detail. It means the 300-agent orchestration architecture is inspectable.</p>
<p>Most proprietary multi-agent systems are black boxes. You can observe their outputs, but you can’t examine how they decompose tasks, how they handle failures, or what the coordination protocol looks like between agents. Open-source releases change that.</p>
<p>The <a href="/blog/what-is-minimax-m2-7-self-evolving-ai">MiniMax M2.7 self-optimization architecture</a> is another case where the open release mattered more than the benchmark numbers — because it let researchers examine the recursive self-improvement mechanism directly rather than inferring it from outputs.</p>

<p>With Kimi K2, the same principle applies. Researchers and engineers can study how Moonshot AI solved the 300-agent coordination problem. That’s a contribution to the field that doesn’t show up in any benchmark table.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-8"><h2 id="s-8">What to Watch</h2>
<p>The immediate question is whether the Kimi K2 orchestration architecture gets adopted, forked, or improved by the open-source community. The model weights matter less than whether the coordination layer proves useful outside Moonshot AI’s specific use cases.</p>
<p>Watch for papers and blog posts analyzing the coordination protocol specifically. The benchmark comparisons will keep coming — they’re easy to produce. The architecture analysis will take longer but will be more useful.</p>
<p>If you’re building agents that need to decompose large tasks into parallel workstreams, the Kimi K2 release is worth studying directly rather than through the benchmark coverage. The <a href="/blog/gemma-4-vs-qwen-3-6-plus-agentic-workflows">Gemma 4 vs Qwen 3.6 Plus comparison for agentic workflows</a> covers some of the model-level tradeoffs for open-weight options, but the Kimi K2 architecture question is upstream of model selection — it’s about how you structure the work before you decide which model handles each piece.</p>
<p>The secondary question is what the Hermes content ideation pattern implies for research workflows. An agent running on a $0.24/hour CPU instance, given a vague prompt about overlooked AI stories, surfaced a technically significant architecture detail that most human coverage missed. That’s not a fluke — it’s a repeatable capability. The prompt was <em>“Research what’s going on in AI and give me three fresh content ideas that most people have overlooked but are important.”</em> You could run that prompt today.</p>
<p>For teams building research or monitoring workflows on top of agent infrastructure, this is the pattern worth replicating. Not the specific use case, but the structure: continuous autonomous research, weighted toward signal that falls outside standard coverage templates, delivered through whatever messaging integration fits your workflow (Telegram, Discord, Slack — Hermes supports all three out of the box).</p>
<p>The Kimi K2 story is a good example of what that looks like in practice. An execution substrate, not a better model. Open source. Running 300 agents across 4,000 steps. The benchmark coverage will fade. The architecture will matter for longer.</p>

          var index = c.indexOf("=");
          var key   = c.slice(0, index);
          var value = c.slice(index + 1);
          return [ decodeURIComponent(key), decodeURIComponent(value) ];
        }));
        var userData = false;
        for (var key in cookies) {
          if (key.startsWith('CognitoIdentityServiceProvider') && (key.endsWith('.idToken') || key.endsWith('.accessToken'))) {
            userData = true;
          }
        }
        return userData;
      };
      try {
        window.__mindstudioWebflowAuthData = getAuthData();
      } catch (e) {}
    </script><!-- Signed-in / Signed-out Visibility --><script>
      function updateElementVisibility() {
        var isLoggedIn = !!window.__mindstudioWebflowAuthData;
        var elements = document.querySelectorAll('[visibility]');
        elements.forEach(function (element) {
          var value = element.getAttribute('visibility');
          if (isLoggedIn) {
            if (value === 'signed-out') element.classList.add('mindstudio-hidden');
            if (value === 'signed-in') element.classList.remove('mindstudio-hidden');
          } else {
            if (value === 'signed-out') element.classList.remove('mindstudio-hidden');
            if (value === 'signed-in') element.classList.add('mindstudio-hidden');
          }
        });
      }
      updateElementVisibility();
      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', updateElementVisibility);
    </script><!-- Attribution Data --><script>
      var localStorageKey = 'mindstudio_attributionData';
      var cookieKey = 'mindstudio_attributionData';

      function setAttrCookie(name, value, days) {
        var date = new Date();
        date.setTime(date.getTime() + days * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000);
        var expires = 'expires=' + date.toUTCString();
        document.cookie = name + '=' + encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(value)) + ';' + expires + ';path=/;domain=.mindstudio.ai;Secure;SameSite=Lax';
      }

      function getAttrCookie(name, jsonParse) {
        var nameEQ = name + '=';
        var cookies = document.cookie.split(';');
        for (var i = 0; i < cookies.length; i++) {
          var c = cookies[i];
          while (c.charAt(0) === ' ') c = c.substring(1, c.length);
          if (c.indexOf(nameEQ) === 0) {
            var contents = decodeURIComponent(c.substring(nameEQ.length, c.length));
            return jsonParse ? JSON.parse(contents) : contents;
          }
        }
        return null;
      }

      function setAttrLocalStorage(name, value) {
        localStorage.setItem(name, JSON.stringify(value));
      }

      function getAttrLocalStorage(name) {
        var item = localStorage.getItem(name);
        return item ? JSON.parse(item) : null;
      }

      function isConsentToAnalytics() {
        var consentCookie = getAttrCookie('cookieyes-consent');
        if (!consentCookie) return false;
        return consentCookie.includes('analytics:yes');
      }

      function storeAttributionData() {
        var params = new URL(window.document.location.href).searchParams;
        var utmSource = params.get('utm-source') || params.get('utm_source') || '';
        var inviteCode = params.get('invite-code') || '';
        var partnerStackId = params.get('ps_xid') || '';
        var partnerStackKey = params.get('ps_partner_key') || '';
        var partnerStackCoupon = params.get('ps_coupon') || '';
        var referrer = window.document.location.href;

        var conversionParams = {
          facebook: { fbclid: params.get('fbclid') || '' },
          google: { gclid: params.get('gclid') || '', wbraid: params.get('wbraid') || '', gbraid: params.get('gbraid') || '' },
          linkedin: { li_fat_id: params.get('li_fat_id') || '' },
          reddit: { rdt_cid: params.get('rdt_cid') || '' }
        };

        var existingAttribution = isConsentToAnalytics()
          ? getAttrCookie(cookieKey, true)
          : getAttrLocalStorage(localStorageKey);

        function mergeConversionParams(existing, incoming) {
          var merged = {};
          var allKeys = new Set([].concat(Object.keys(existing || {}), Object.keys(incoming || {})));
          allKeys.forEach(function (platform) {
            merged[platform] = {};
            var existPlatform = (existing && existing[platform]) || {};
            var incomingPlatform = (incoming && incoming[platform]) || {};
            var paramKeys = new Set([].concat(Object.keys(existPlatform), Object.keys(incomingPlatform)));
            paramKeys.forEach(function (key) {
              merged[platform][key] = incomingPlatform[key] || existPlatform[key] || '';
            });
          });
          return merged;
        }

        var attributionData;
        if (existingAttribution) {
          attributionData = Object.assign({}, existingAttribution, {
            referrer: referrer || existingAttribution.referrer,
            inviteCode: inviteCode || existingAttribution.inviteCode,
            partnerStackId: partnerStackId || existingAttribution.partnerStackId,
            partnerStackKey: partnerStackKey || existingAttribution.partnerStackKey,
            partnerStackCoupon: partnerStackCoupon || existingAttribution.partnerStackCoupon,
            conversionParams: mergeConversionParams(existingAttribution.conversionParams, conversionParams)
          });
        } else {
          attributionData = {
            utmSource: utmSource,
            inviteCode: inviteCode,
            partnerStackId: partnerStackId,
            partnerStackKey: partnerStackKey,
            partnerStackCoupon: partnerStackCoupon,
            referrer: referrer,
            initialLandingPage: window.document.location.href,
            initialReferrer: window.document.referrer,
            conversionParams: conversionParams,
            trialIntent: false
          };
        }

        if (isConsentToAnalytics()) {
          setAttrCookie(cookieKey, attributionData, 30);
        } else {
          setAttrLocalStorage(localStorageKey, attributionData);
        }
      }

      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function () {
        try { storeAttributionData(); } catch (e) { console.log(e); }
      });

      document.addEventListener('cookieyes_consent_update', function (eventData) {
        try {
          if (eventData.detail.accepted.includes('analytics')) {
            var attributionData = getAttrLocalStorage(localStorageKey);
            if (attributionData) {
              setAttrCookie(cookieKey, attributionData, 30);
              localStorage.removeItem(localStorageKey);
            }
          }
        } catch (e) { console.log(e); }
      });
    </script><!-- Persist Query Params Across Links --><script>
      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function () {
        var search = window.location.search;
        if (!search) return;

        var params = new URLSearchParams(search);
        if (!params.toString()) return;

        var links = document.querySelectorAll('a[href]');
        links.forEach(function (link) {
          var href = link.getAttribute('href');
          if (!href || href.startsWith('#') || href.startsWith('mailto:') || href.startsWith('tel:') || href.startsWith('javascript:')) return;

          try {
            var url = new URL(href, window.location.origin);
            params.forEach(function (value, key) {
              if (!url.searchParams.has(key)) {
                url.searchParams.set(key, value);
              }
            });

            if (href.startsWith('/') || href.startsWith('./') || href.startsWith('../') || !href.includes('://')) {
              link.setAttribute('href', url.pathname + url.search + url.hash);
            } else {
              link.setAttribute('href', url.toString());
            }
          } catch (e) {}
        });
      });
    </script><!-- A/B Testing: Inject experiment assignments --><script>(function(){const experimentAssignments = "{}";

      window.__msExperiments = JSON.parse(experimentAssignments);
    })();</script><!-- A/B Testing: Fire Experiment View events --><script>
      if (window.__msExperiments && typeof window.plausible === 'function') {
        Object.keys(window.__msExperiments).forEach(function (expId) {
          window.plausible('Experiment View', {
            props: {
              experiment: expId,
              variant: window.__msExperiments[expId],
              page: window.location.pathname,
            },
          });
        });
      }
    </script><!-- Plausible Custom Events: CTA Clicks + Scroll Depth --><script>
      (function () {
        // CTA Click tracking — fires on elements with data-cta attribute
        document.addEventListener('click', function (e) {
          var el = e.target.closest('[data-cta]');
          if (!el || typeof window.plausible !== 'function') return;
          var ctaName = el.getAttribute('data-cta');
          if (!ctaName) return;
          var ctaProps = {
            cta_name: ctaName,
            cta_location: el.getAttribute('data-cta-location') || 'unknown',
            page: window.location.pathname,
          };
          var ctaFormat = el.getAttribute('data-cta-format');
          if (ctaFormat) ctaProps.cta_format = ctaFormat;
          var ctaAngle = el.getAttribute('data-cta-angle');
          if (ctaAngle) ctaProps.cta_angle = ctaAngle;
          var ctaVariantId = el.getAttribute('data-cta-variant-id');
          if (ctaVariantId) ctaProps.cta_variant_id = ctaVariantId;
          var ctaCopyIndex = el.getAttribute('data-cta-copy-index');
          if (ctaCopyIndex) ctaProps.cta_copy_index = ctaCopyIndex;
          var ctaSlotIndex = el.getAttribute('data-cta-slot-index');
          if (ctaSlotIndex) ctaProps.cta_slot_index = ctaSlotIndex;
          if (window.__msExperiments) {
            Object.keys(window.__msExperiments).forEach(function (expId) {
              ctaProps['exp_' + expId.replace(/-/g, '_')] = window.__msExperiments[expId];
            });
          }
          window.plausible('CTA Click', { props: ctaProps });
        });

        // Scroll Depth tracking — fires at 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% milestones
        var fired = {};
        var thresholds = [25, 50, 75, 100];
        function checkScroll() {
          var docHeight = document.documentElement.scrollHeight - window.innerHeight;
          if (docHeight <= 0) return;
          var pct = Math.round((window.scrollY / docHeight) * 100);
          for (var i = 0; i < thresholds.length; i++) {
            var t = thresholds[i];
            if (pct >= t && !fired[t] && typeof window.plausible === 'function') {
              fired[t] = true;
              window.plausible('Scroll Depth', {
                props: { depth: t + '%', page: window.location.pathname },
              });
            }
          }
        }
        window.addEventListener('scroll', checkScroll, { passive: true });
        // Check once on load for short pages
        window.addEventListener('load', checkScroll);
      })();
    </script><!-- Vercel Web Analytics --><script defer="" src="/_vercel/insights/script.js"></script></body></html>
